title: MLX (Apple Silicon)
lesson:
MLX is a software library, built by Apple, for running and training machine-learning models on Apple Silicon — the M-series chips (M1, M2, M3, M4, and so on) inside modern Macs. A library here just means a toolbox of ready-made building blocks you call from Python (and Swift). MLX exists for one reason: regular AI tools were written for Nvidia graphics cards, and Macs do not have those. MLX is Apple's answer — a toolbox that speaks the Mac's own language and squeezes real AI performance out of the hardware you already own.

The one trick that makes Macs special: unified memory

To get why MLX matters, you need one idea: unified memory. On a typical gaming PC the processor (CPU) and the graphics card (GPU) each have their own separate pool of memory. The GPU's private memory is called VRAM, and it is usually small and expensive.

To run a model, you must copy its weights from the CPU's memory across to the GPU's VRAM — and if the model is too big for that small pool, it simply will not fit.

Think of it like a kitchen with two cooks who each have their own fridge. Every ingredient one cook needs has to be carried over from the other's fridge — slow, and limited by whichever fridge is smaller. Apple Silicon instead gives both cooks one shared fridge: the CPU and GPU read from the same single pool of memory, with no copying.

On a Mac with 64 GB of unified memory, the GPU can use almost all 64 GB. That is the headline: a $2,000 laptop can hold a model that would need a $10,000 server GPU, because there is no tiny separate VRAM wall to hit. MLX is the tool written specifically to exploit that shared fridge.

What MLX actually gives you

MLX comes in two parts:

  • The core array library — a low-level math toolbox that feels almost exactly like NumPy or PyTorch (the popular Python math libraries), so people who know those feel at home immediately.
  • MLX-LM — a ready-to-use package for language models that downloads, runs, and fine-tunes them in a couple of commands. Most beginners only ever touch this part.

Running a chat model is one line:

pip install mlx-lm
mlx_lm.generate --model mlx-community/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-4bit \
  --prompt "Explain unified memory in one sentence."

That mlx-community name is a Hugging Face account (see Hugging Face) holding hundreds of models already converted to MLX's format and pre-quantized — quantization means storing each weight with fewer bits to save memory, like vacuum-packing clothes so they fit a smaller suitcase. The 4bit in the name means it has been packed down to roughly 4 bits per weight.

A second analogy: speaking the local dialect

Other local-AI tools work on Macs too. llama.cpp and Ollama run perfectly well there. The difference is that MLX is like a person who grew up speaking the Mac's native dialect, while the others are skilled translators. Translators do a fine job, but a native speaker catches nuances they miss.

MLX is built around Apple's own Metal graphics engine and the chip's design, so for many models it runs faster and uses memory more tightly than the cross-platform tools. The trade-off: MLX only speaks to Apple Silicon. Run it on a Windows PC or an Nvidia box and it does nothing — those need vLLM or llama.cpp instead.

It trains too, not just runs

MLX's quiet superpower is fine-tuning on the same laptop. Because of unified memory, you can teach a model new behaviour using LoRA — a cheap method that trains only a tiny set of extra weights instead of the whole model — without ever renting a cloud GPU. One command kicks it off:

mlx_lm.lora --model mlx-community/Mistral-7B-v0.3-4bit \
  --train --data ./my_data --iters 600

💡 Rule of thumb: take your Mac's memory in GB, keep a few GB free for the operating system, and the rest is roughly the largest 4-bit model you can run. 16 GB comfortably runs a 7–8B model; 64 GB reaches into 70B territory.

So MLX's place in the world is clear: it is the best-fitting tool for doing serious local AI — both running and fine-tuning — when your machine is a Mac, turning everyday Apple laptops into surprisingly capable AI workstations.
mentalModel: MLX is a native AI toolbox for Mac chips. Its magic is unified memory: instead of the CPU and GPU each owning a small separate fridge and constantly carrying ingredients between them, Apple Silicon gives them one big shared fridge — so an everyday Mac can hold and run models that would otherwise need an expensive server GPU.
takeaways: MLX is Apple's native AI library — Apple Silicon Macs only.
Unified memory shares one pool, so big models fit a laptop.
MLX-LM runs and LoRA-fine-tunes models in one command.
mistakes: Expecting MLX to work on non-Apple hardware. MLX only runs on Apple Silicon (M-series) Macs. On Windows, Linux, or any Nvidia/AMD machine it is useless — reach for vLLM or llama.cpp there instead.

Trying to load a raw Hugging Face model and wondering why it is slow or broken. MLX prefers models in its own format. Grab pre-converted ones from the mlx-community account, or convert with mlx_lm.convert first, rather than pointing it at an unconverted PyTorch checkpoint.

Assuming all your unified memory is free for the model. macOS, your browser, and other apps are also eating that shared pool. Leave several GB of headroom; a 16 GB Mac cannot actually devote the full 16 GB to a model without swapping and stalling.

Confusing MLX with Ollama or llama.cpp. They overlap (all run local models on a Mac) but MLX is Apple-native and also does training; Ollama is a friendly model manager; llama.cpp is the cross-platform engine. Pick MLX when you want maximum Mac performance or want to fine-tune locally.

Grabbing the heaviest quantization by default. An 8-bit model is sharper but may not fit alongside the OS; a 4-bit version usually runs nearly as well and leaves room to breathe. Start at 4-bit and only move up if you see real quality loss.

Believing a Mac can fine-tune anything a cloud GPU can. Unified memory is generous, but large full fine-tunes still need cloud hardware. On a Mac, stick to LoRA-style training on small-to-mid models, not full training of a 70B.
exercise:
On an Apple Silicon Mac, install MLX-LM (pip install mlx-lm) and run a small chat model from the mlx-community account — for example a 3B or 8B instruct model at 4-bit. Ask it the same prompt three times and, in another terminal tab, watch memory usage (Activity Monitor or top) while it answers. Note how many gigabytes the model occupies and roughly how fast tokens stream out.

Stretch goal: Make a tiny training file of 20–30 example question-and-answer pairs in your own voice, then run mlx_lm.lora --train on a small model to fine-tune a LoRA adapter. Generate from the tuned model and check whether its style shifted toward your examples.
solution: Hint: A 4-bit 8B model lands around 4.5–5 GB in memory and should stream comfortably fast on an M-series chip (often dozens of tokens per second). If the machine crawls, you are probably out of free memory and the system is swapping (shuffling data to disk) — try a smaller model or a lower-bit version. For the stretch goal, keep --iters modest (a few hundred) and the model small (3B–7B); you should see the tuned model start echoing the phrasing and tone of your examples even from a tiny dataset, which is exactly what a LoRA adapter is for. If nothing changes, your data file format is likely wrong — MLX-LM expects a specific JSONL layout, so check the docs for the exact field names.
summary: MLX is Apple's native AI library for Apple Silicon Macs. Its key advantage is unified memory — one shared pool for CPU and GPU — letting everyday Macs hold models that would otherwise need pricey server GPUs. The MLX-LM package runs and fine-tunes language models in a line or two, using pre-converted, quantized models from the mlx-community account. Choose MLX over Ollama or llama.cpp when you want top Mac performance or local LoRA fine-tuning; it does nothing on non-Apple hardware.

diagram.caption: A PC must copy weights into a small separate VRAM pool, while Apple Silicon lets CPU and GPU share one large memory pool — so MLX can fit much bigger models.

quiz.q: What is the single feature of Apple Silicon that lets a Mac run models a normal laptop GPU could not?
options: A faster clock speed on the CPU
Unified memory shared by the CPU and GPU
quiz.explain: Unified memory means the CPU and GPU read from one large shared pool with no copying, so there is no tiny separate VRAM wall to hit. That is exactly why a 64 GB Mac can hold very large models.

quiz.q: Your friend wants to run MLX on their Windows desktop with an Nvidia GPU. What should you tell them?
options: Install MLX-LM and it will use the Nvidia GPU
MLX only runs on Apple Silicon; use vLLM or llama.cpp instead
MLX works but only on the CPU there
MLX runs anywhere as long as Python is installed
quiz.explain: MLX is built around Apple's Metal engine and only runs on Apple Silicon Macs. On Windows or Nvidia hardware they should reach for vLLM or llama.cpp.

quiz.q: You point MLX-LM at a raw, unconverted PyTorch checkpoint and it is slow or fails. What is the most likely fix?
options: Use a model in MLX's format — from mlx-community or via mlx_lm.convert
quiz.explain: MLX prefers models in its own format. Grab pre-converted ones from the mlx-community Hugging Face account, or run mlx_lm.convert first, instead of feeding it a raw checkpoint.

quiz.q: On a 16 GB Mac, why is a 4-bit model usually the safer first choice over an 8-bit one?
options: 8-bit models cannot run on Apple Silicon at all
macOS and other apps also share the pool, so 4-bit leaves headroom to avoid swapping
4-bit models train faster on the cloud
quiz.explain: The OS, browser, and other apps eat into the same unified pool, so an 8-bit model may not fit without swapping to disk and stalling. A 4-bit version runs nearly as well and leaves breathing room.

keyTerms.def: Apple's open-source machine-learning library for Apple Silicon Macs. A toolbox (called from Python or Swift) for running and training models, built to use the chip's Metal engine and unified memory.
Apple's own line of chips (M1, M2, M3, M4 and up) that combine the CPU, GPU, and memory in one design. MLX runs only on these chips, not on Intel Macs or other hardware.
A single shared pool of memory that both the CPU and GPU read from directly, with no copying between them — the trait that lets Macs run large models without a separate, small VRAM pool.
The higher-level MLX package for language models. It downloads, runs, and fine-tunes models with simple commands like mlx_lm.generate and mlx_lm.lora.
A Hugging Face account holding hundreds of popular models already converted to MLX's format and pre-quantized, so you can run them on a Mac without converting anything yourself.
Apple's low-level graphics and compute engine, the Mac equivalent of Nvidia's CUDA. MLX talks to the GPU through Metal, which is why it is so well tuned for Apple chips.
Storing each model weight with fewer bits (e.g. 4-bit instead of 16-bit) to shrink memory use, like vacuum-packing clothes. MLX models often come pre-quantized; a name ending in 4bit is one example.
Low-Rank Adaptation: a cheap fine-tuning method that trains a tiny set of extra weights instead of the whole model, making it feasible to teach a model new behaviour on a single Mac.
